entities.name: IMPROVED SEEDS
entities.description: Seeds developed through research to provide better agricultural outcomes

entities.name: SMALLHOLDER FARMERS
entities.description: Farmers operating small-scale farms who adopt improved seed varieties

entities.name: RESEARCH METHODS
entities.description: Techniques used to analyze and understand farmers' seed demand

entities.name: FARMERS SEED DEMAND
entities.description: The demand and preferences of farmers for specific seeds

entities.name: SEED AVAILABILITY
entities.description: The accessibility of seeds to farmers in their local context

entities.name: THEORY OF SOCIAL LIFE OF METHODS
entities.description: A theory suggesting that research methods influence the reality they are meant to study

entities.name: SOCIAL POSITION
entities.description: The social status of a farmer which influences seed access and attractiveness

entities.name: CROP
entities.description: Agricultural plants grown within a farming system

entities.name: FARMING SYSTEM
entities.description: The organizational structure of agricultural production on a farm

entities.name: MARKET
entities.description: The economic system for the acquisition and sale of seeds

entities.name: AGRO-ECOLOGICAL CONDITIONS
entities.description: Environmental and ecological factors affecting seed performance and choice

entities.name: FARMERS PREFERENCES AND NEEDS
entities.description: The specific requirements and desires of farmers regarding seed characteristics

entities.name: SEED SYSTEMS
entities.description: The overall network of seed production, distribution, and use

entities.name: HOLISTIC METHODOLOGICAL RESEARCH APPROACH
entities.description: An integrated research method designed to capture the variable real-life context of farmers

entities.name: SEED ACTORS
entities.description: Stakeholders involved in the seed value chain

entities.name: POLICYMAKERS
entities.description: Individuals who create policies to enable informed decisions in seed systems

relationships.source: THEORY OF SOCIAL LIFE OF METHODS
relationships.description: supports the premise that they shape outcomes
